summary: Built the code-quality guardrails as small custom checks because the off-the-shelf checker couldn't read the same-line 'this is allowed here' notes the rules rely on.
detail: The plan describes the sequential-by-default and blocking-call gates using ESLint's stock 'no-restricted-syntax' rule. That stock rule cannot inspect a flagged line's own trailing allow-comment, but the leaf's binding tests require exactly that (a same-line // allow:, // allow-sync:, or // allow-broad-catch: comment makes the construct pass). Implemented three custom comment-keyed rules in eslint-plugin-loom-local instead; Adds. prose is descriptive/illustrative while the comment-keyed pass/fail behaviour is the binding obligation, so the custom rules are the faithful implementation.

task: Extension factory shell and end-to-end harness
summary: Made the extension's startup code refuse to crash by simply skipping any failed setup step, leaving the smarter 'which failures are fatal' rules and error reporting for a later piece of work.
detail: The integration spec says some startup registration failures (the command-line flag, the event subscriptions) should be fatal and abort the rest of startup, while others (the message renderer) should be skipped and startup continues. This leaf's binding requirement is only that startup never throws, runs each registration under its own try/catch, and returns synchronously. So the factory swallows-and-continues on every failed call rather than implementing the per-call fatal-vs-continue distinction or the bootstrap-failed diagnostics; those, plus the capability-probe refusal logic, are explicitly deferred to leaf V9a per the leaf text. The narrower behaviour keeps the never-throw boundary faithful while not inventing V9a's contract here.

summary: Defined how the build checks that diagnostic codes never get silently renamed by comparing the current list against a frozen baseline list of code names.
detail: The diagnostics spec rule (DIAG-3) says renaming a diagnostic code is a breaking change deferred to a future major release, but it does not say HOW the automated gate should detect a rename (and ordinary additions/removals ARE allowed in the current release). The tests pin the narrowest reasonable mechanism: a pinned baseline list of code names, with the gate flagging any baseline name that is no longer present in the current registry as a rename. This is a design choice the spec leaves open, recorded here because it constrains the paired implementation.

summary: When turning a host error into a one-line diagnostic message, the code defends against the rare case where even reading the error's text fails, and falls back to a fixed 'unreadable' label.
detail: The diagnostics spec requires that if converting a caught error value to a string itself throws (a hostile error object), the renderer emit the literal text <unreadable> instead of crashing. There is no way to know in advance whether that conversion will throw without attempting it, so this needs a catch that accepts any thrown value — which the project's 'specific exception types only' rule bans except at five named places. I classified this catch as the allowed 'Pi SDK boundary' site, because every error value reaching this code was caught from a Pi host call whose shape loom cannot trust, and tagged it with the sanctioned // allow-broad-catch: pi-sdk-boundary comment. The catch only substitutes the fixed sentinel; it does not hide any other behaviour.
